summary: DeVault argues that open source users who want bugs fixed or features added should learn to do it themselves rather than expecting volunteer maintainers to prioritize their needs. He explains that contributors to his projects are self-directed and work on what interests them, not what users demand. The post pushes back against users who claim they 'can't' contribute by pointing out the abundance of learning resources available. He frames the refusal to try as a 'defeatist attitude' rather than a genuine inability, arguing that helping users become contributors is a better investment than doing the work for them.
key_points: Open source maintainers and contributors are self-directed volunteers, not a business making a sale — they have no obligation to prioritize user demands
Users who claim they 'can't' fix their own problems are conflating unfamiliarity with inability — not knowing a language or library is a starting point, not a barrier
Mentoring a user into becoming a contributor yields better long-term returns than fixing their problem directly, even if mentoring takes more time
The open source ecosystem provides abundant resources (documentation, Q&A sites, chat rooms) that make self-directed learning viable for motivated users
money_quotes: If you want something changed, send a patch. If you are running into an annoying bug, fix it and send a patch. If you want a new feature, implement it and send a patch.
We aren't a business. We aren't making a sale. We're just making cool software that works for us and publishing it in the hopes that you'll find it useful, too.
You don't learn new things by hanging out in your comfort zone.
We would much rather give you advice on how to fix the problem yourself than to fix the problem for you.
Even if it takes more of our attention to do so, we get the added benefit of a new person who is qualified to help out the next guy.
The cards are stacked in your favor. The only problem is your defeatist attitude.
key_insight: Open source thrives when users stop waiting for someone else to solve their problems and instead invest in becoming contributors themselves.
